picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet reported $109.9B in Q1 2026 revenue (+22% YoY) with Google Cloud posting $20B — up 63% YoY, decisively refuting the AI cannibalization bear thesis. The $460B cloud backlog (up from $240B at end of Q4 2025) is the single most forward-looking demand signal of the entire earnings season, and EPS estimates have been revised +5.2% since January. Bill Ackman holds GOOGL at ~12.5% of Pershing Square, confirming institutional smart-money backing, and the stock is the strongest Mag 7 performer over the last 12 months on a relative basis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) with Google Cloud trajectory and $460B backlog conversion — any acceleration above 63% YoY growth rate would be a major upside catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks (late July earnings)
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~26x forward 2026 EPS — only a modest premium to S&P 500's ~20.9x given 22%+ revenue growth and expanding Cloud margins; consolidating above key support after a strong post-earnings run
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY, cloud backlog stops growing, or AI search cannibalization evidence emerges in Search revenue deceleration below 10% YoY
picks.keyRisk: AI-driven disruption to Google Search's advertising revenue model from generative AI competitors (Perplexity, OpenAI ChatGPT search)

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom is experiencing 106% YoY revenue growth in AI semiconductors, reported $19.31B in Q1 FY2026 revenue (+29%), and guided Q2 to $22B — implying 47% YoY growth. The company extended its partnership with Meta to deploy technology supporting multi-gigawatts of Meta's custom MTIA silicon, and expanded its Google Cloud collaboration, cementing its position as the dominant hyperscaler custom ASIC platform. With a 2T market cap, 29 analyst Strong Buy ratings, and a $451 consensus price target, AVGO has doubled over the past 12 months while beating the S&P 500 across every major time horizon.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings on June 3, 2026 — the key data point is whether AI revenue hits the $10.7B guide and whether Q3 guidance confirms a continued ASIC supercycle above 50% YoY growth
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks (June 3 earnings)
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$427, off its recent 52-week high of $437.68; P/E of ~82x is elevated but justified by 106% AI semiconductor revenue growth and operating margins above 60% on ASIC business — pullback from highs offers a cleaner entry
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a major hyperscaler ASIC design win (Google TPU, Meta MTIA, or a third major contract), or AI semiconductor revenue growth decelerates below 50% YoY in the Q2 print
picks.keyRisk: Marvell Technology emerging as a competing ASIC platform for Alphabet's next-generation TPU cycle, directly displacing Broadcom revenue

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Financials are the single most important sector rotation signal today — XLF moved into positive 5-day relative strength while Energy shed -3.26% on Iran deal hopes, and Schwab's May 2026 outlook highlights that modest yield curve steepening and structural digitalization trends are driving improved net interest income and capital markets revenues. Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley reported strong Q1 earnings confirming the financial industry upcycle, and a steepening 2s10s spread of +49bps (trending toward the long-term median of +88bps) structurally widens NIMs and capital markets profitability. Druckenmiller's largest Q4 buy was XLF, validating the sector rotation thesis.
picks.catalyst: Continued yield curve steepening toward the +88bps long-run median, plus Q2 2026 earnings in mid-July showing capital markets (M&A advisory, trading) revenue acceleration driven by corporate deal-making revival
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2-3 months (June-July)
picks.entryNote: GS trading at approximately 13-14x forward earnings — a significant discount to the market's 20.9x forward P/E despite ROE consistently above 15%; Financials are rotating into leadership on a 5-day basis as of today's session
picks.exitTrigger: Yield curve re-inverts, HY OAS widens above 400bps signaling credit stress, or DOJ antitrust action disrupts capital markets activity
picks.keyRisk: Credit cycle deterioration — HY OAS has already widened from 264bps to 328bps; a further widening above 400bps would signal loan loss provisioning and hit earnings

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY has de-rated meaningfully from its 2024 peak (52-week range $623.78–$1,133.95; trading near ~$971 today), offering a materially better entry on the same GLP-1 structural thesis: Mounjaro/Zepbound commands the dominant obesity/diabetes treatment franchise globally with a decade-long demand runway. At ~35x forward P/E (vs. 50x+ at peak), valuation is far more compelling than a year ago. Bill Baruch bought LLY today per CNBC flow tracker, confirming continued active accumulation. In a late-cycle/stagflation regime, LLY's defensive-growth profile — low beta of 0.48, secular earnings growth, minimal rate sensitivity — makes it a superior risk-adjusted hold.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings and updated Zepbound/Mounjaro supply ramp data — manufacturing capacity has been the binding constraint; any evidence of supply normalization restores the revenue trajectory and re-rates shares toward $1,200+
picks.catalystWindow: Next 6-8 weeks (Q2 earnings late July/early August)
picks.entryNote: At ~$971 vs. 52-week high of $1,133.95, LLY is trading ~14% off its highs with a beta of 0.48 and forward P/E of ~35x — a material de-rating from 50x+ peak multiples while the GLP-1 franchise only grew stronger; analyst targets cluster between $1,183–$1,500
picks.exitTrigger: GLP-1 competitive moat eroded by Novo Nordisk (NVO) gaining formulary preference, or FDA action on a safety signal that materially limits the obesity indication
picks.keyRisk: Drug pricing policy risk — any legislative action capping GLP-1 pricing or restricting Medicare/Medicaid coverage would directly compress revenue projections

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon's Q1 2026 earnings were a blowout: net sales of $181.5B (+17% YoY), EPS of $2.78 vs. $1.64 expected (+69% beat), and AWS reaccelerating to 28% growth — its fastest pace in 15 quarters. Bedrock AI processed more tokens in Q1 2026 than in all prior years combined, with customer spend growing 170% QoQ. Bill Ackman added +65% to his AMZN position in Q4 2025, making it his second-largest holding at ~14.3% of Pershing Square. Berkshire trimmed AMZN (profit-taking), but Ackman's aggressive addition reinforces the structural AI cloud + retail margin expansion thesis.
picks.catalyst: AWS Q2 2026 revenue trajectory — the key question is whether the 28% acceleration continues toward 30%+ in Q2, as supply constraints on AI compute ease with new data center capacity coming online
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks (Q2 earnings late July)
picks.entryNote: AMZN trading at ~$276.82; the 69% EPS beat creates a new earnings baseline that meaningfully compresses the forward multiple; AWS at a $150B+ run rate growing 28% YoY makes this structurally undervalued relative to pure-play cloud peers
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 20% YoY, retail operating margins compress below 5%, or regulatory action breaks up AWS from the retail business
picks.keyRisk: AI capex bubble deflation — AMZN is spending heavily on AI infrastructure; if enterprise AI ROI fails to materialize in H2 2026, AWS guidance cuts cascade and compress the multiple

picks.name: Microsoft Corporation
picks.rationale: Microsoft reported Azure growth of 40% in its most recent quarter, topping the 39.3% StreetAccount estimate, with Q2 guidance for 39-40% growth (above consensus of 37%) and management signaling supply — not demand — is the binding constraint for the third consecutive quarter. EPS of $4.27 beat by $0.21. This supply-constrained demand setup creates a self-reinforcing acceleration thesis into H2 2026 as new data center capacity comes online. EPS revisions are positive with consensus up ~1.2%, providing stable revision momentum into the next reporting cycle.
picks.catalyst: Azure H2 2026 acceleration signal at the June analyst day or in Q4 FY2026 earnings (expected late July) — management's third consecutive 'supply-constrained' commentary validates a demand floor, and any supply normalization unlocks the upside
picks.catalystWindow: Next 6-10 weeks (Q4 FY2026 earnings late July)
picks.entryNote: MSFT at ~$413 trades at approximately 31x forward EPS — elevated but below peak 2024 multiples of 35x+; Azure's supply-demand imbalance is a structural moat signal rarely seen in large-cap tech
picks.exitTrigger: Azure growth decelerates below 30% YoY, Copilot enterprise monetization stalls with weak seat penetration data, or regulatory forced separation of Teams/Office from cloud
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler AI capex spending reversal — if enterprise AI ROI disappoints in H2 2026, Azure and all hyperscaler cloud peers face guidance cuts simultaneously

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: Even as Iran ceasefire talks push WTI below $100 today (-3.26% for XLE), XOM is the highest-quality, lowest-cost operator in the Energy sector with FCF yields of 8-12% at current oil prices and a structural advantage from its Permian Basin low-cost production. Analysts project 71% Energy sector earnings growth in Q2 2026 and 35%+ for the next four quarters — a powerful earnings upgrade cycle that supports the position even if oil mean-reverts toward $85-90. Berkshire's Occidental Petroleum stake confirms the smart-money energy thesis; XOM is the less volatile, higher-quality expression. A ceasefire reduces the geopolitical premium but not the structural supply-constrained energy thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July — with WTI near $94-100/bbl average for the quarter, XOM should report record-level FCF; any Middle East re-escalation is an asymmetric positive, while a ceasefire would reduce but not eliminate the thesis
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks (Q2 earnings late July)
picks.entryNote: XOM trades at 8-12x FCF at current oil — deep value versus the market's 20.9x forward P/E; the ceasefire-driven selloff today creates a better entry point for a name that was up ~29% YTD as of last week
picks.exitTrigger: WTI crude oil sustained below $70/bbl for 60+ consecutive days, eliminating the FCF thesis; or a full US-Iran peace deal causing structural oil supply restoration above 3M bbl/day
picks.keyRisk: A durable US-Iran ceasefire that fully restores Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic, causing a rapid $20-25/bbl oil price decline that compresses XOM FCF and multiple simultaneously

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: Vistra reports Q1 2026 results on May 7 (tomorrow) with consensus revenue of $5.4B (+38.5% YoY) and EPS expected to show a 380% increase year-over-year. It holds strategic 20-year nuclear PPAs with Amazon (1,200 MW at Comanche Peak) and Meta (2,176 MW + 433 MW uprates) — contracted, visible cash flow that insulates it from commodity price swings. Vistra is the largest single contributor to Utilities sector earnings growth in Q1 2026 per FactSet. The AI data center power demand supercycle is a multi-decade secular tailwind directly benefiting VST's nuclear + gas fleet in PJM and ERCOT markets.
picks.catalyst: Q1 2026 earnings on May 7, 2026 — the first clean read on whether contracted nuclear PPA revenues are flowing through, and whether management raises FY guidance given accelerating AI data center power demand
picks.catalystWindow: Next 48 hours (May 7 earnings); then ongoing data center contract announcements
picks.entryNote: VST is near 52-week highs driven by AI power demand; valuation is elevated on a trailing basis but reasonable on 2026-2027 contracted FCF given $4.07B cash from operations in 2025 — tomorrow's earnings are the key reset event
picks.exitTrigger: Q1 earnings miss on the ~380% EPS growth estimate by more than 30%, cancellation or renegotiation of the Amazon or Meta nuclear PPAs, or ERCOT/PJM regulatory caps on power pricing
picks.keyRisk: Earnings volatility tied to commodity prices — VST missed Q4 2025 EPS by 76%, demonstrating lumpy near-term earnings; a repeat miss tomorrow could trigger a sharp derating

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: Defense is a structural beneficiary of the Iran war and elevated geopolitical risk, with increased NATO/allied defense spending representing a multi-year orders tailwind. RTX (Raytheon + Pratt & Whitney + Collins Aerospace) combines missile defense, jet engines, and avionics — three growing defense verticals. Industrials are up ~12% YTD vastly outperforming the broader market, and defense sub-sector spending is largely insulated from tariff headwinds and consumer cyclicality. Even if the Iran ceasefire materializes, the broader geopolitical re-armament cycle (Ukraine, Taiwan, NATO 2% GDP commitments) sustains long-cycle defense orders through 2028+.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July with order backlog update — Raytheon missile and air defense systems have seen elevated demand tied to the Iran conflict; any backlog acceleration above $200B signals multi-year earnings visibility
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks (Q2 earnings late July); ongoing geopolitical demand signals
picks.entryNote: RTX trades at approximately 18-20x forward EPS — a discount to the market at 20.9x despite secular defense spending growth; the name was a strong early 2026 performer and has pulled back modestly from highs, offering a cleaner entry
picks.exitTrigger: Pratt & Whitney GTF engine fleet inspection costs reescalate materially above $7B provision, US defense budget sequestration cuts Raytheon missile procurement by 20%+, or durable Middle East peace reduces global arms demand
picks.keyRisk: Pratt & Whitney GTF engine powder metal disk inspection program — ongoing fleet maintenance liability that represents a material ongoing cash cost and earnings headwind if scope expands

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: S&P Global is a high-quality financial data and ratings compounder with near-monopoly pricing power in credit ratings, indices, and market intelligence. As yield curve steepening encourages corporate bond issuance and M&A activity recovers, SPGI's ratings revenue — highly correlated to debt issuance volumes — benefits structurally. With 84% of S&P 500 companies beating EPS estimates in Q1 2026, the investment banking and corporate finance activity that drives ratings revenue is accelerating. SPGI commands durable 40%+ EBITDA margins, making it one of the highest-quality earnings compounders in the Financials universe with minimal credit cycle risk (it rates debt, it doesn't hold it).
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July — debt issuance volumes and M&A advisory fee recovery should drive ratings revenue acceleration; any Fed signaling toward a September cut would turbocharge bond issuance and SPGI ratings revenue
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks (Q2 earnings); September Fed meeting expectations
picks.entryNote: SPGI trades at approximately 28-30x forward earnings — a premium multiple justified by its monopolistic market position in financial data and 40%+ EBITDA margins; recent underperformance relative to broader Financials creates a catch-up opportunity
picks.exitTrigger: Antitrust action forcing divestiture of IHS Markit or Platts, a structural collapse in debt issuance volumes below 2020 levels, or loss of SEC recognition as a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization
picks.keyRisk: Stagflation scenario where corporate debt issuance collapses due to prohibitively high rates, directly cutting SPGI's ratings revenue by 15-25%

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the cleanest macro hedge in the current stagflation-adjacent regime: real rates are near zero on a backward-looking basis, central bank demand provides a structural bid, and the Iran war geopolitical risk premium — even partially — supports elevated gold prices. J.P. Morgan noted gold reaching $5,000/oz as a tail-risk scenario, and State Street's chief investment strategist described gold as posting 'outstanding' performance in 2026. With the dollar under pressure from widening fiscal deficits and a potential Warsh-era Fed leadership shift introducing policy uncertainty, gold serves as the portfolio's primary hedge against both inflation persistence and institutional risk repricing.
picks.catalyst: April CPI print on May 12 — a hot print (above 3.5% YoY) would re-accelerate the stagflation narrative and drive institutional inflows into gold as a real asset hedge; Iranian deal breakdown or ceasefire collapse is an asymmetric upside catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: Next 1 week (May 12 CPI); ongoing geopolitical signals
picks.entryNote: GLD at multi-month highs reflecting geopolitical premium; however, the structural macro case (near-zero real rates, central bank accumulation, USD debasement risk) supports holding even at elevated prices — this is insurance, not a trade
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained positive real rates above 2% (Fed funds rate well above core PCE), US fiscal credibility restored via material deficit reduction, and full Middle East peace deal removing geopolitical premium
picks.keyRisk: A durable US-Iran ceasefire combined with a hot payrolls print accelerating Fed hike odds, driving real rates sharply positive and compressing gold by 10-15%

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury ETF
picks.rationale: With the 10-year yield at 4.38% and the 2s10s spread at +49bps versus a long-run median of +88bps, IEF provides 4.3% carry while you wait and functions as the portfolio's primary equity-drawdown hedge. Bonds are likely to be an effective hedge against the largest risks to equities — a recession or AI-optimism reversal — particularly at intermediate maturities. If the Iran ceasefire materializes and oil drops, the inflation outlook improves, increasing the probability of a September Fed cut and driving bond price appreciation. IEF outperforms TLT in a stagflation scenario due to lower duration, making it the superior defensive fixed income vehicle in this regime.
picks.catalyst: April CPI print on May 12 — a soft print opens the September cut window and drives IEF price appreciation; Iran ceasefire progress would reduce energy-driven inflation expectations and steepen the curve favorably for intermediate duration
picks.catalystWindow: Next 1-2 weeks (May 12 CPI); June 16-17 FOMC meeting
picks.entryNote: 10-year at 4.38% offers 4.3% carry — above the S&P 500 earnings yield of 3.82% — making IEF the rational incremental capital destination over equities at the margin; intermediate duration (~7yr) balances inflation risk vs. recession hedge
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year yield breaks above 5.0% on a sustained basis (evidence the bond market is pricing in structural inflation above 4%), or FOMC explicitly adopts a hiking bias under incoming Chair Warsh
picks.keyRisk: Warsh-led Fed adopts a hawkish balance sheet normalization stance, aggressively cutting the balance sheet and driving term premium higher — pushing 10-year yields above 4.75-5.0%

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides diversified exposure to high-quality dividend compounders — concentrated in Financials, Industrials, Consumer Staples, and Healthcare — with a sub-1 beta, approximately 3.5% dividend yield, and low expense ratio. In the current regime where the earnings yield on the S&P 500 (3.82%) is below the 10-year Treasury (4.38%), dividend-focused quality names with stable cash flows outperform on a risk-adjusted basis by offering income without taking excessive multiple-compression risk. A non-recessionary rate-cutting cycle is historically constructive for U.S. earnings and dividend-paying equity performance, meaning SCHD benefits from either a soft landing or a moderate inflation retreat.
picks.catalyst: Any Fed easing signal — even a single 25bp cut language shift — drives income equity re-rating; Q2 2026 dividend increases from SCHD's underlying holdings (Chevron, Home Depot, Merck) provide a compounding income stream
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2-6 months (ongoing income + any dovish Fed pivot signal)
picks.entryNote: SCHD near historical fair value with a 3.5% dividend yield; significantly lower volatility than pure equity positions — functions as the portfolio's ballast ETF replacing single-stock consumer staples risk at stretched multiples (Costco at 48x P/E)
picks.exitTrigger: Credit spreads blow out above 500bps HY OAS causing dividend cuts across underlying holdings, or a sustained deflationary recession causes broad earnings deterioration across dividend-paying sectors
picks.keyRisk: A sudden stagflationary recession that simultaneously pressures consumer staples and financial earnings, cutting dividends across SCHD's top holdings and reducing NAV

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire's $370B+ record cash pile is itself the portfolio's most powerful put option — historically, Berkshire's large cash positions have preceded major market dislocations followed by significant capital deployment. Q1 2026 operating earnings rose to $11.35B from $9.64B, confirming the business engine is fully intact even as Greg Abel transitions to CEO. Berkshire's diversified exposure across insurance, utilities, energy, consumer staples, and rails means it outperforms in virtually every equity drawdown scenario since 2000. At a modest premium to book with sub-market volatility, BRK.B is the highest-quality defensive equity anchor in the universe.
picks.catalyst: Q1 2026 13F filing due May 15 — Greg Abel's first independent capital allocation decisions will be revealed, potentially showing a large acquisition or deployment of the $370B cash pile into distressed opportunities; any major acquisition announcement is a high-conviction positive catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: Next 9 days (May 15 13F); ongoing capital deployment monitoring
picks.entryNote: BRK.B trades at approximately 1.5-1.6x book value — historically fair for Berkshire; the record $370B cash position creates an embedded optionality premium that the market is not fully pricing given typical buyback-per-book discipline
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes a large (>$100B) acquisition at a materially overvalued price, permanently impairing the capital allocation quality signal; or a sustained insurance combined ratio above 100 for 4+ consecutive quarters indicating underwriting discipline deterioration
picks.keyRisk: Abel deviates from Buffett's capital allocation discipline with an overpriced acquisition, destroying the book value per share compounding engine that justifies the premium to intrinsic value

picks.name: NextEra Energy Inc.
picks.rationale: NextEra couples rate-base stability with the single most credible renewables growth lever in the utility sector, and AI data center power demand is creating a multi-decade structural tailwind for its Florida regulated utility and NextEra Energy Resources renewable development pipeline. Domestically-focused power infrastructure players benefit from AI data center expansion, industrial reshoring, and the energy transition per Schwab's May 2026 sector analysis. NEE provides the portfolio's utility defensive exposure with a growth kicker — distinct from VST's more volatile commodity-linked earnings. Non-cyclical demand characteristics provide defensive positioning during economic uncertainty.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July — the key data point is whether NextEra Energy Resources signs additional AI data center power purchase agreements (PPAs) with hyperscalers, extending the contracted backlog beyond the current $100B+ development pipeline
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10-12 weeks (Q2 earnings); ongoing data center PPA announcements
picks.entryNote: NEE trades at approximately 19-21x forward EPS — slightly above the utility sector historical average of 15x but justified by the AI power demand secular growth premium; technically consolidating off 52-week highs, offering a better risk/reward than the prior year's extended levels
picks.exitTrigger: Florida rate case denial or significant PUC rate reduction exceeding 10% of requested returns, offshore wind project impairments materially above $1B, or 10-year yield sustainably above 5.0% compressing utility multiples
picks.keyRisk: Rate sensitivity — NEE's elevated valuation relative to utility history means a sustained 10-year yield above 5.0% (from the current 4.38%) would compress the multiple by 15-20% independent of fundamental performance

summary: The macro regime remains late-cycle stagflation-adjacent — Fed on hold at 3.5-3.75%, core PCE at 3.2%, real GDP at 2.0% SAAR, and the Warsh confirmation vote imminent — but today's most important development is an intraday regime signal: oil briefly broke below $100 on Iran ceasefire hopes, Energy is down -3.26% as of this morning (the worst sector), and Financials are rotating to +1.04% leadership. The growth book maintains the high-conviction GOOGL, AVGO, GS, LLY quartet from prior days — all theses intact with strong Q1 earnings beats and positive estimate revisions — while adding AMZN (the cleanest hyperscaler momentum compounder at a post-earnings reset), MSFT (supply-constrained Azure demand), XOM (best-in-class energy value at discounted entry post-ceasefire selloff), VST (AI power demand compounder reporting tomorrow), RTX (defense secular spend), and SPGI (Financials rotation quality compounder). The defensive sleeve spans gold (GLD — stagflation/geopolitical hedge), intermediate treasuries (IEF — equity drawdown hedge with 4.3% carry), dividend equity (SCHD — income ballast), Berkshire (BRK.B — record cash optionality), and NextEra (NEE — regulated utility + AI power growth). The dominant risk underwritten is an AI capex bubble deflation where enterprise AI ROI fails to materialize in H2 2026, simultaneously pressuring GOOGL Cloud, AVGO ASICs, MSFT Azure, and AMZN AWS guidance — a scenario partially hedged by the 22% defensive sleeve weighting and GLD's non-correlation to tech multiples.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans six GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, MSFT), Financials (GS, SPGI), Health Care (LLY), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Energy (XOM), Utilities (VST), and Industrials (RTX) — well within the 3-per-sector and 5-sector minimum constraints; AVGO and MSFT share AI infrastructure exposure but operate at complementary points in the value chain (custom ASICs vs. cloud platform). The defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold commodity (GLD), intermediate-duration US Treasuries (IEF), broad dividend equity (SCHD), and regulated utility with growth (NEE), plus the fortress conglomerate (BRK.B) — explicitly avoiding a double-up in any single defensive category.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc. Class A

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom is the single most compelling AI infrastructure compounder in the semiconductor space with differentiated exposure: AI semiconductor revenue hit $8.4B (+106% YoY) in Q1 2026 with Q2 guidance projecting ~140% YoY growth, and the CEO has targeted $100B in AI chip revenue by 2027. The stock is up ~127% over the past year but is being added to by institutional holders (Alecta Pensionsforsakring boosted its stake by 398,900 shares in late April 2026), confirming accumulation at current levels.

picks.catalystWindow: Early June 2026 (next 5 weeks)

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD is the mandatory late-cycle/stagflationary hedge in this portfolio. Gold is trading at ~$4,621/oz as of May 4, 2026, with J.P. Morgan projecting prices toward $5,000/oz by Q4 2026, and Wells Fargo targeting $6,100–$6,300 by year-end. The macro backdrop is near-perfect for gold: the Iran/Strait of Hormuz conflict is unresolved with the US maintaining its naval blockade, the DXY has weakened to ~98.60, fiscal deficits are running at 5.9% of GDP (expanding toward 7–7.5%), and the equity risk premium has flipped negative. GLD has returned 8.47% YTD vs SPY's 5.16%, and central banks bought 244 tonnes in Q1 2026 alone (+3% YoY), with the World Gold Council affirming that geopolitics and investment demand will remain the primary support structure. This is the portfolio's tail-risk buffer against both a stagflation trap (scenario 1) and a Treasury market stress event (scenario 2) — the only asset that benefits from both simultaneously.
picks.catalyst: Iran/Strait of Hormuz escalation (military briefing to Trump confirmed); Kevin Warsh Fed Chair confirmation (May 15) signaling hawkish pivot that pressures DXY; April CPI upside print (May 12)
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2–6 weeks (May–June 2026)
picks.entryNote: GLD at ~$148–$152 per share (underlying gold ~$4,600/oz); after correcting from the January 2026 high of $5,405/oz, gold is in consolidation mid-range — the WGC notes investment demand far exceeds fabrication demand; this is a strategic, not tactical, position — sized at 20% as a portfolio hedge
picks.exitTrigger: Comprehensive Iran nuclear deal signed AND Strait of Hormuz fully reopened AND 10-year yield falls below 3.5% (eliminating stagflation premium) — the three-way resolution that removes all three tailwinds simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: A 'risk-on' relief rally driven by a surprise Iran peace deal could temporarily knock gold 10–15%, as energy prices fall and the stagflation premium deflates; sized accordingly as a hedge, not a pure return driver

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro strategy briefing as of **Monday, May 4, 2026**.

---

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO DASHBOARD — May 4, 2026

---

### FED POLICY


The Fed held the federal funds rate steady at the **3.50%–3.75%** target range for a 2nd consecutive meeting in March 2026, in line with expectations.
 
The Fed is widely expected to keep the rate unchanged at the 3.50%–3.75% range for a third consecutive meeting in April 2026, as policymakers navigate an increasingly complex environment.


**Dot plot (March 2026):** 
The March 2026 Fed dot plot projected a **3.00%–3.25%** fed funds rate by year-end 2027.
 
Policymakers still signaled one reduction in the fed funds rate this year and another in 2027, though the timing remains unclear.
 **Market-implied path:** 
The outlook for the rest of the year remains uncertain, with oil prices rising and inflation picking up due to the energy shock — markets currently expect **no changes to rates this year**.


**Real policy rate vs. neutral:** 
With the policy rate close to neutral, the IMF cautioned that there is little room to cut interest rates in 2026, particularly given the rise in energy prices, the likely passthrough to core inflation, and upside risks to global commodity prices.
 
The Fed has cut rates by **175 bps** in total since September 2024.


---

### YIELD CURVE


As of May 1, 2026: **10y = 4.39%**, **2y = 3.88%**, **30y = 4.97%**.
 The **2s10s spread = ~+51 bps** — positively sloped, confirming curve re-steepening in progress. 
The 3m10y spread was deeply negative from October 2022 to December 2024, and since February 2026 has been swinging back and forth from positive to negative territory.
 
The 10-year yield remains sharply higher this year as inflation threats on a robust economy erased bets of a Fed cut in 2026.
 
Treasury yields have been trending higher since the onset of the Middle East conflict, driven by concerns that elevated energy prices could reignite inflation.
 **Term premium direction: rising** — the long end is bearing the brunt of energy/fiscal concerns, with the 30y at nearly 5%.

---

### INFLATION


Core PCE prices **accelerated** in March.
 
The Fed projects PCE inflation to remain elevated, with PCE inflation expected to end 2026 at **2.7%**, 30 bps higher than the December 2025 projection.
 
Inflation moved sideways during 2025 as tariffs boosted goods prices while services inflation moderated.
 
ISM Manufacturing prices paid soared to a **4-year high** in April, as companies experienced higher energy costs in the wake of the Iran war alongside elevated tariff charges.
 Trajectory: **re-accelerating** on a goods/energy basis, with supercore (core services ex-shelter) sticky due to tight labor market. 
The median FOMC participant revised inflation projections upward for 2026 and 2027, though still expects return to target by 2028.


---

### GROWTH


US consumption slowed in Q1 2026, though surging AI investment supported **+2.0% annualized GDP expansion** in Q1.
 
GDP growth is expected to accelerate modestly in 2026 to **2.4%** (Q4/Q4 basis).


**Payrolls run-rate:** 
The US economy added **178K jobs** in March 2026, the most since December 2024, coming in well above the market expectation of 60K.
 The Jan–Mar 3-month run-rate is roughly **+68K/month** (dragged by the strike-distorted Feb print of -133K). 
Over the year, average hourly earnings have increased by **3.5%**.


**ISM:** 
The April ISM Manufacturing index totaled **52.7**, unchanged from March and slightly below expectations; the prices paid component surged to its highest level since April 2022, driven by higher energy costs and tariff charges.


---

### CREDIT & LIQUIDITY


Following three hawkish dissents at the April Fed meeting, core PCE acceleration and energy-driven ISM prices signal a tightening bias that bears watching for credit spreads.
 HY OAS data from FRED's ICE BofA index is directionally widening amid the Middle East risk premium, though not yet at distressed levels. The 30y Treasury at ~4.97% and fiscal deficits 
of 5.9% of GDP in FY2025
 continue to pressure the long end. 
The general government deficit is expected to remain in the **7–7.5% of GDP** range with debt exceeding 140% of GDP by 2031.
 **DXY:** The dollar has firmed modestly on "higher for longer" repricing and geopolitical safe-haven demand.

---

### EARNINGS BACKDROP


The S&P 500 trailing earnings yield stood at **3.82%** as of May 1, 2026
, implying a **trailing P/E of ~26.2x** — materially above the 10-year average of ~19–20x. 
The forward earnings yield with estimates was **4.10%** as of year-end 2026 projections
, implying a **forward P/E of ~24.4x**. The equity risk premium (earnings yield minus 10y at 4.39%) is **negative at ~-58 bps** — stocks are priced for perfection relative to bonds, with bonds now offering a meaningful competing yield. 
The S&P 500 was heading for its best month since 2020 in April, with Apple earnings on deck
 — a relief rally following earlier volatility.

---

### GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE


Iran delivered a new proposal for the US to consider after President Trump rejected previous concessions and pledged to maintain the naval blockade, which together with Iran's threats has suspended energy and goods exports from the Middle East.
 
Crude oil remains on track for a second consecutive weekly gain amid fading hopes for a US–Iran peace deal and expectations that the Strait of Hormuz will remain closed near-term.
 
The April FOMC meeting could mark Powell's final meeting as Chair — the Justice Department said it would halt its criminal investigation into Powell, removing a key obstacle to the Senate's confirmation of his nominated successor, **Kevin Warsh**, whose appointment is set for **May 15**.


---

## 📋 REGIME BRIEFING — 3 PARAGRAPHS

---

### 1. 🔴 REGIME CALL: **Late-Cycle / Transition Zone**

This is a **late-cycle regime with stagflationary overtones** — the economy is expanding (2% real GDP, 52.7 ISM Mfg, robust labor market), but inflation is **re-accelerating** courtesy of an energy shock from the Iran conflict and residual tariff pass-through, 
with the IMF explicitly warning that the policy rate is close to neutral, leaving little room to cut
, while 
three hawkish dissents at the April FOMC meeting signal the committee is leaning against easing.
 The curve is bear-steepening (30y at 4.97%), the equity risk premium has flipped negative, and markets have fully priced out 2026 cuts — the classic late-cycle fingerprint of a Fed trapped between sticky inflation and a resilient economy.

---

### 2. ✅ WHAT'S WORKING / ❌ WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** Energy (direct beneficiary of the Iran supply shock — oil producers, MLP infrastructure), **Defense & Aerospace** (geopolitical spending premium), **Value/Dividend equities** (higher long yields compress duration-sensitive growth, rotating capital to cash-flow-heavy names), **Healthcare** (defensive earnings, 
healthcare added 76K jobs in March, the strongest sectoral print
, signaling sector strength), and **Commodities/Materials** (inflation re-acceleration + supply disruption). **AI/Infrastructure Capex** remains supported — 
surging AI-related investment helped sustain GDP growth in Q1.


**Breaking:** Long-duration growth equities (negative ERP, 10y at 4.39% competes directly with ~4.1% forward earnings yield), 
Financial Services (financial activities employment is down 77K since May 2025
), Consumer Discretionary (consumption slowed in Q1 under elevated gasoline/energy prices), and REITs (30y mortgage rate at 6.30%, cap rates pressure). Rate-sensitive small caps are also under pressure from the higher-for-longer repricing.

---

### 3. 👁️ WHAT TO WATCH — Top 2–3 Catalysts (Next 1–6 Months)

1. **Kevin Warsh Fed Chair Confirmation (~May 15):** 
Warsh's Senate confirmation appointment is set for May 15
 — his arrival could signal a more hawkish institutional tone and shift the Fed's reaction function. Any statement suggesting tolerance for higher inflation targets *or* faster rate hikes would be the single largest near-term repricing event for equities and long bonds.

2. **Iran/Strait of Hormuz Resolution or Escalation:** 
Long-term yields eased as oil and product prices whipsawed from their peaks, despite the impasse between Iran and the US that prolonged the suspension of energy exports from the key region.
 A deal that reopens exports would immediately knock 10–15% off crude, relief-rally the long end, and rotate capital from Energy/Defense back into rate-sensitives. A military escalation has the opposite effect — stagflation premium spikes.

3. **April CPI (due mid-May) & April Nonfarm Payrolls (May 2):** 
The prices paid component of ISM Manufacturing surged to a four-year high
, pre-signaling upside CPI risk. If April CPI prints ≥0.4% MoM core alongside a payrolls beat (above 150K), the remaining market pricing for any 2026 cut will evaporate entirely — and the 10y could test 4.60–4.75%, a genuine headwind for a market trading at 24x forward earnings with a negative equity risk premium.

---
*All data as of Friday May 1 / weekend of May 2–4, 2026. Sources: Federal Reserve, BLS, US Treasury, IMF Article IV, Trading Economics, Advisor Perspectives, GuruFocus, CNBC.*

Tesla's stock has underperformed all of its megacap peers so far this year as global competition ramps up in the electric vehicle market.

**Verdict: –25 to –40% risk on enterprise IT spending pullback or competitive displacement.**

Gold is expected to see continued strength as reallocations to the asset class, especially from central banks, continue.
 
Stagflationary risks and a potentially weaker dollar may prompt global investors to rethink high exposures to US assets. While it is premature to forecast the dollar's demise, there is potential upside for gold.
 Gold has near-zero equity beta in risk-off regimes, serves as a USD hedge, and benefits from central bank accumulation.

Berkshire is a fortress: massive cash reserves (north of $300B as of last count), no single-stock earnings dependency, diversified across insurance, utilities, energy, consumer staples, and rails.
